entry.title: Learning Journey: Applying SAP Digital Manufacturing
entry.content:
There's a learning journey on SAP Learning that I want to highlight: Applying SAP Digital Manufacturing.

If you're working with SAP Digital Manufacturing as a consultant or business user, this is a structured path through everything you need to know to position, implement, and operate the solution.

It includes 11 intermediate level courses, and leads to the Achievement Badge below:

Learning Scope

  • Understanding how to position SAP Digital Manufacturing
  • Getting an overview about SAP Digital Manufacturing and SAP Digital Manufacturing for Insights
  • Understanding how to collect manufacturing data and how to create a data collection concept
  • Configuring master data for discrete-, and process manufacturing
  • Configuring and analyzing master data for discrete-, and process  manufacturing
  • Understanding the execution of shopfloor control processes in the discrete-, and process manufacturing area using SAP Digital Manufacturing
  • Exploring the extensibility mechanisms in SAP Digital Manufacturing
  • understanding and configuring the shop floor integration with SAP Digital Manufacturing to enable machine connectivity
  • Understand how to set up and connect the on-premise applications SAP Production Connector and SAP Cloud Connector required for machine connectivity with the cloud application SAP Digital Manufacturing

What it covers

The learning journey walks you through the full scope of SAP DM, from the ground up:

  • Positioning and overview — You can build a solid understanding of the SAP Digital Manufacturing platform and its Insights component, so you know how it fits into a broader manufacturing landscape.

  • Data collection strategy — You can learn how manufacturing data collection works and how to approach strategy development for your specific environment.

  • Master data configuration — You can configure master data for both discrete and process manufacturing, two distinct manufacturing models with different requirements.

  • Shopfloor execution — You can execute shopfloor control processes end-to-end, which is where most of the day-to-day manufacturing work happens.

  • Extensibility — You can explore the extensibility mechanisms available within SAP DM to adapt the platform to specific business needs.

  • Machine connectivity — You can set up shop floor integration including machine connectivity, using SAP Production Connector and SAP Cloud Connector to bridge on-premise systems with the cloud.

Who it's for

The learning journey is designed for consultants and business users working with SAP Digital Manufacturing. The only prerequisite is basic knowledge of planning and manufacturing, so you don't need to be a deep technical expert to get started.

Why it matters

SAP Digital Manufacturing covers a wide surface area: from how production orders flow through the shop floor, to how machines report data, to how you configure the system for a process plant versus a discrete factory. Having a structured learning path that ties all of that together in one place saves a lot of time compared to piecing it together from documentation and trial and error.

If you're new to SAP DM or want to fill gaps in your knowledge, this is a good place to start.

Explore the learning journey here: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/applying-sap-digital-manufacturing

Stay tuned for more! Thanks,
Manoel Costa

entry.title: Brazil Tax Reform - How should the CBS/IBS Credit Option be determined for Business Partners?
entry.content:
Hello,

We are implementing the Brazil Tax Reform standard SAP notes and have a question regarding the determination of the CBS/IBS Credit Option (Regular Regime vs. Simplified Regime).

During our analysis, we found that:

The standard already provides the CRT (Código de Regime Tributário) field for both Suppliers (LFA1-CRTN) and Customers (KNA1-CRTN).
SAP Note 3646289 introduces the CBS/IBS Tax Situation field in the maintenance view J_1BTAXCODEV (table J_1BT007), indicating that part of the tax determination is driven by Customizing.
However, we could not find any standard field or configuration to store or determine the Credit Option (Regular Regime / Simplified Regime) for Business Partners.

Our questions are:

Is SAP planning to deliver a standard field for the CBS/IBS Credit Option in the Business Partner (Customer/Supplier) master data?
Is this information expected to be derived from the existing CRT field?
Or should the Credit Option be determined exclusively through the CBS/IBS Tax Situation maintained in J_1BTAXCODEV?
Are there any upcoming SAP Notes or roadmap items addressing this requirement?

We reviewed SAP Note 3646289 and the Brazil Tax Reform documentation but could not identify guidance for this specific scenario.

Any clarification from SAP or from customers already implementing the Tax Reform would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you!

entry.title: API_MRP_MATERIALS_SRV_01
entry.content: Hello,In S/4HANA Public Cloud, I need to retrieve Material Planning data and consume it in a custom BTP application intended for deployment in the public cloud.While researching the available options, I identified the following API as the appropriate one for this use case:Plain TextAPI_MRP_MATERIALS_SRV_01I was able to successfully retrieve the data using Postman. However, when I added this service to the manifest.json file of the BTP app project and attempted to deploy the app, I encountered an error stating that the usage of this service is forbidden in this context.Based on my investigation, the issue appears to be that this service is configured as an inbound service, whereas my intended use case requires it to be configured as outbound.Could you please advise on the following:Is there a way to resolve this error and correctly enable outbound usage of this service?Alternatively, is there a released Data Definition (e.g., a CDS view or equivalent API) that exposes the same Material Planning data and supports outbound consumption?Thank you in advance for your help.Best regards,

entry.title: Integrating GitHub with SAP Integration Suite: A Complete Guide to Version Control and DevOps
entry.content:
Connect GitHub with SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration)

Introduction

In today's integration landscape, organizations are increasingly adopting DevOps practices and CI/CD pipelines to accelerate software delivery while maintaining governance and quality. One of the key enablers of DevOps is a robust Version Control System that allows teams to collaborate efficiently, track changes, and manage application lifecycle effectively.

SAP Integration Suite, Cloud Integration (formerly known as SAP CPI), provides native integration with Git-based repositories such as GitHub. This capability allows integration developers to store, version, and manage integration artifacts directly in a Git repository, bringing modern source control practices into the integration development lifecycle.

By integrating GitHub with SAP Integration Suite, organizations can:

  • Maintain version history of integration artifacts.

  • Enable collaboration among multiple developers.

  • Support branching and release management strategies.

  • Implement CI/CD pipelines.

  • Maintain backup and recovery mechanisms.

  • Improve governance and auditability of changes.

In this blog, we will walk through the complete process of:

  • Creating a GitHub Personal Access Token.

  • Creating and configuring a GitHub repository.

  • Establishing connectivity between SAP Integration Suite and GitHub.

  • Performing Git operations such as Push, Pull, and Import.

  • Understanding best practices for managing integration artifacts using Git.

Why Use GitHub with SAP Integration Suite?

Traditionally, integration artifacts were managed directly within SAP Integration Suite, making collaboration and version management challenging in larger projects.

Git integration solves these challenges by introducing capabilities such as:

Feature Benefit

Version ControlTrack every modification to integration artifacts
CollaborationMultiple developers can work simultaneously
Backup & RecoveryRepository acts as a central backup
GovernanceBetter control using branching strategies
AuditabilityComplete history of changes
CI/CD IntegrationEnables automated deployment pipelines

Git integration becomes extremely useful in enterprise projects where multiple developers work across various environments such as DEV, QA, UAT, and PROD.

Architecture Overview

SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration)
                |
                | HTTPS + Personal Access Token
                |
           GitHub Repository
                |
      ----------------------
      |         |          |
    main      develop    feature/*

Integration artifacts such as Integration Flows, Script Collections, Value Mappings, and Message Mappings are stored inside Git repositories and can be synchronized between SAP Integration Suite and GitHub.

Prerequisites

Before proceeding, ensure the following prerequisites are met.

SAP Prerequisites

  • Access to SAP Integration Suite – Cloud Integration.

  • User must have the PI_Administrator role assigned.

  • Appropriate authorizations to create and modify integration artifacts.

GitHub Prerequisites

  • A valid GitHub account.

  • Internet connectivity to access GitHub.

  • Permission to create repositories and generate Personal Access Tokens.

Step 1 – Create a Personal Access Token in GitHub

SAP Integration Suite communicates with GitHub using Git APIs. Since GitHub no longer supports password-based authentication for Git operations, authentication must be performed using a Personal Access Token (PAT).

The Personal Access Token acts as a secure credential that authorizes SAP Integration Suite to perform operations such as:

  • Read repository information

  • Push integration artifacts

  • Pull latest changes

  • Import artifacts from repositories

Navigate to GitHub Settings

Login to GitHub and navigate to:

Profile → Settings

 

Open Developer Settings

Navigate to:

Settings → Developer Settings

 

Create a Personal Access Token

Navigate to:

Personal Access Tokens → Tokens (Classic)

Click:

Generate New Token (Classic)

 

Configure the Token

Provide:

  • Token Name

  • Expiration Date

  • Required Permissions (Scopes)

For testing purposes, all repository-related scopes can be selected. However, in productive environments, it is recommended to grant only the minimum permissions required by following the Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP).

Typical scopes include:

  • repo

  • workflow

  • read:org

  • Etc

 

 

Important Note

The generated token is displayed only once by GitHub.

Ensure that you copy and securely store the token because it cannot be viewed again once the page is closed.

This token will be used while configuring Git access in SAP Integration Suite.

Step 2 – Create a Repository

A Git repository acts as the central storage location for all integration artifacts.

Every time an artifact is pushed from SAP Integration Suite, GitHub stores:

  • Source code

  • Version history

  • Commit information

  • Metadata

  • Change logs

This repository can then be used for:

  • Collaboration

  • Backup

  • Recovery

  • Audit purposes

  • CI/CD integration

Navigate to the GitHub home page and click:

New Repository

Provide:

  • Repository Name

  • Description

  • Visibility (Public or Private)

For enterprise projects, it is recommended to use Private repositories since integration artifacts may contain business logic and sensitive information.

Example:

SAP-CPI-Integration-Artifacts

Step 3 – Create a Branch

Git uses branches to isolate development activities.

Since SAP Integration Suite performs operations against a particular branch, at least one branch must exist.

The easiest way to create the default branch is:

  1. Create a README file.

  2. Commit the file.

GitHub automatically creates the main branch.

 

Why Branching is Important

A proper branching strategy enables:

  • Parallel development

  • Controlled releases

  • Easier rollback

  • Better governance

  • Reduced conflicts

A typical enterprise branching strategy looks like:

Branch Purpose

mainProduction-ready artifacts
developIntegration testing
feature/*Development activities
releasePre-production validation
hotfixProduction bug fixes

GitHub Setup Completed

At this stage, we have successfully completed all the necessary configurations on GitHub.

The next step is to establish connectivity between GitHub and SAP Integration Suite.

Step 4 – Configure Git Access in SAP Integration Suite

Navigate to:

Settings → Git Repositories

SAP Integration Suite provides a centralized location for managing Git repository connections.

As of now, SAP Integration Suite allows configuring up to 50 Git repository connections.

This capability is useful when organizations maintain:

  • Project-specific repositories

  • Shared repositories

  • Customer-specific repositories

  • Environment-specific repositories

Click:

Edit → Add

Provide:

Field Value

Repository URLGitHub Repository URL
Access TokenGitHub Personal Access Token

Click:

Add → Save

Once saved, SAP Integration Suite validates the credentials and establishes a secure connection with GitHub.

 

Git Operations in SAP Integration Suite

After the repository connection is established, SAP Integration Suite supports three major Git operations:

  1. Git Push

  2. Git Pull

  3. Git Import

Git Push

The Git Push operation uploads integration artifacts from SAP Integration Suite into GitHub.

This operation is generally performed when:

  • A new artifact is created.

  • Existing artifacts are modified.

  • Development work is completed.

  • Changes need to be backed up.

Navigate to:

Actions → Git Push

Provide:

  • Access Token

  • Repository

  • Branch

  • Commit Message

 

 

Example commit messages:

Initial version of Customer Integration Interface
Added retry mechanism for SFTP processing
Enhanced mapping for Sales Order Interface

Benefits of Git Push

  • Creates version history.

  • Enables collaboration.

  • Maintains backup.

  • Supports CI/CD pipelines.

  • Improves governance.

After a successful push, Git information becomes visible in the integration artifact.

 

Git Pull

Git Pull is used to synchronize the latest version of the artifact from GitHub into SAP Integration Suite.

This is useful when:

  • Another developer has modified the artifact.

  • Changes have been merged into the repository.

  • Local artifacts need to be updated.

Navigate to:

Actions → Git Pull

Provide:

  • Access Token

  • Repository

  • Branch

 

Important Consideration

Pull operations can fail due to version conflicts.

For example:

  • Local artifact is already modified.

  • Repository version is different.

  • Local version is ahead of repository version.

Therefore, it is recommended to perform Pull operations regularly before starting development.

Git Import

Git Import allows developers to import artifacts directly from Git repositories into another package or tenant.

This feature is extremely useful in scenarios such as:

Tenant Migration

Moving integration artifacts between tenants.

Reusability

Importing common frameworks and templates.

Project Onboarding

New team members can quickly access existing interfaces.

Disaster Recovery

Rebuilding an Integration Suite tenant from repository content.

Navigate to:

Actions → Git Import

Provide:

  • Access Token

  • Repository

  • Branch

SAP Integration Suite displays all available artifacts in the selected branch.

Choose:

  • Add

  • Add and Open in Editor

depending on your requirement.

 

 

Recommended Git Strategy for SAP Integration Suite

A recommended branching model is:

main      → Production
release   → UAT
develop   → SIT
feature/* → Development

This approach provides:

  • Better governance

  • Parallel development

  • Easier rollback

  • Controlled deployments

  • Reduced production risks

Best Practices

Use Feature Branches

Avoid developing directly on the main branch.

Commit Frequently

Small commits are easier to track and troubleshoot.

Write Meaningful Commit Messages

Commit messages should explain the technical or business change.

Push Before Deployment

Always push the latest changes to Git before deploying to higher environments.

Protect Main Branch

Allow deployments to production only through approved pull requests and code reviews.

Use Private Repositories

Integration artifacts may contain sensitive business logic and should be protected appropriately.

Conclusion

GitHub integration with SAP Integration Suite introduces modern version control capabilities into the integration development lifecycle. By leveraging Git operations such as Push, Pull, and Import, organizations can establish a robust DevOps process, improve collaboration among developers, and maintain better governance over integration artifacts.

Integrating SAP Integration Suite with GitHub is therefore a highly recommended practice for organizations aiming to build scalable, maintainable, and enterprise-ready integration solutions while embracing modern software delivery practices.

Special thanks to @ShraddhaDas151  for the continuous support, guidance, and valuable insights that contributed to the preparation of this blog.

Thank you for reading this blog. Feel free to share your feedback, suggestions, or questions in the comments section below. Your inputs are highly appreciated and contribute to continuous learning within the SAP Community. Happy integrating!

entry.title: Re: Setting Up Enterprise Organizational Management in S/4HANA Service
entry.content:
Hello,

I am currently reviewing the configuration of PPOSA_CRM / Manage Organizations in the context of the SAP Service module and would appreciate your support in understanding a specific aspect of the setup.

During our implementation, we maintained the address details of the Service Organizational Units, including the country and postal code, to have an automated determination when creating a solution quotation (according to the BP address/postal code).

However, from our testing, it appears that this configuration may restrict transactions to customers or objects within the same country as the Service Organizational Unit.

This behavior raises some questions for us, as it seems quite limiting from a business perspective. It would be surprising if SAP's standard solution only supported service transactions within the country of the assigned organizational unit.

Could you please help clarify the following?

  • Is the maintenance of postal code/address information on the Service Organizational Unit a mandatory requirement for service processing?
  • Does SAP standard use this information for organizational determination, service territory determination, or any other process that would drive such country-based restrictions?
  • Is the observed restriction expected system behavior, or could it indicate a missing or incorrect configuration in our setup?
  • Have you seen scenarios where Service Organizational Units can process transactions across multiple countries without maintaining separate organizational structures per country?

Any guidance, SAP documentation references, or best practices you could share would be highly appreciated, as we are trying to understand whether we are facing a configuration issue or a standard SAP design decision.

Thanks

entry.title: Re: SAP CPQ unable to update category pemissions through api
entry.content:
To to do use this
PUT /api/products/v1/categories/{categoryId}/permission

Body - (json type)

[id1,id2,id3]

Here id1,id2,id3 is the permission groups id (could be market, usertype, brands, permission groups or companies) 

Put call is better as it lets you add multiple permission groups at same time.

If you are creating new category then just use the post call as per the api documentation
and pass on the permission in the body with array [id1, id2, id3]

entry.title: MRRL – Single ERS document for purchase orders containing return items
entry.content:
In the Evaluated Receipt Settlement (ERS) process, using transaction MRRL, when a purchase order includes return items, we need the system to create only one document instead of two separate documents: one invoice and one credit memo.

Currently, when transaction MRRL is executed for a purchase order containing return items, two separate documents are created, even when the “Document Selection per Purchase Order” option is selected.

We would like to avoid this behavior and generate only one document for the entire purchase order.

Is this possible in SAP standard? If so, does it require any specific configuration? Otherwise, would a custom development or enhancement be necessary?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: MRRL – Single ERS document for purchase orders contai...
entry.content:
Due to technical restrictions, it is not possible to create both credits and debits items in the same ERS document.

This is described in note 321721 - MRRL generates separate document for returns.

At code level, this distinction happens in form AUFBAU_ABRECHENBARE_BLOECKE from include LMRMKF0B.
RETPO is among the fields that determine a separate document. Once a RETPO value different from the value in the previous item is found, MRRL is told to create a separate document, even if you activate document selection by PO.

No custom enhancements are possible here, unless you radically change the standard coding at your own risk.

You can suggest a single ERS as a new feature for future SAP releases: 3507575 - Customer Influence channel for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition Procurement

entry.title: Re: MCP Servers on SAP Integration Suite : Build Your First MCP Server from an API
entry.content:
Hi @MikeLegaspi 

Kindly find my response to your queries:

  1. Yes, you can create an MCP Server over any HTTP API, provided you have the API's OpenAPI Specification (OAS). In my next blog, I will cover how to expose an MCP Server over a non-SAP HTTP API.
  2. SAP Integration Suite is the endorsed SAP platform for creating, securing, and managing MCP Servers.

entry.title: SAP Business One Service Layer returns HTTP 500 code 299 when calling a parameterized
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

I am working with SAP Business One on SAP HANA and Service Layer.

I created and exposed a HANA calculation view. The view is correctly published in the SL metadata:

GET /b1s/v2/sml.svc/$metadata

I am calling the view using the following URL:

GET /b1s/v2/sml.svc/VIEWParameters(IP_FECHAFIN=2026-07-31,IP_FECHAINI=2026-07-01)/VIEW

However, Service Layer returns:

{
"error": {
"code": "299",
"details": [
{
"code": "",
"message": ""
}
],
"message": "Internal server error."
}
}

The HTTP status code is: 500 Internal Server Error

Tests already performed

  1. The Service Layer session is valid. Other Service Layer endpoints work correctly.
  2. Other custom SL views without parameters work correctly.
  3. The parameterized calculation view executes correctly directly in SAP HANA Studio using the same date range.
  4. The HANA user has the required permissions.
  5. The view is present in $metadata, including the parameter entity and navigation property.
  6. Both /b1s/v1/sml.svc and /b1s/v2/sml.svc were tested, with the same result.

Additional observation

The SAP Business One Analytics Platform administration page is also showing:

CPU: undefined
Memory: undefined
Disk: undefined
License Server: undefined
Status: 500

The System Logs section of the Analytics administration console displays: Internal Error

Questions

  1. Is the URL syntax correct for a SL calculation view with two Edm.Date parameters?
  2. Is there a known Service Layer issue with HANA calculation-view parameters of type DATE?
  3. Should the parameters be changed from DATE to NVARCHAR and converted with TO_DATE() inside the calculation view?
  4. Could the Analytics Platform status 500 affect parameterized Semantic Layer views?
  5. Which Service Layer or Analytics log contains the actual HANA exception behind Service Layer error code 299?
  6. Is there a known SAP Note or patch related to this behavior?

Thank you.

entry.title: Implementing Dual Schedulers in a Single SAP Integration Suite iFlow: Supporting Ad Hoc and Schedule
entry.content:
Recently, SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) introduced support for multiple Integration Processes in a single iFlow, enabling scenarios like separate scheduled and ad hoc execution paths.

The iFlow below uses two schedulers. The first scheduler is dedicated to ad hoc execution, while the second scheduler handles the regular scheduled execution.

Case 1: Normal Schedule

The iFlow runs as per the configured schedule using the regular scheduler.

 

Case 2: Ad Hoc Run and Automatic Switch Back to Normal Schedule

The iFlow can be triggered for an ad hoc run without modifying the regular scheduler. After the ad hoc execution is completed, the iFlow continues with the normal scheduled run automatically. Ad hoc run happen at 9.12. and as you can see it automatically trgerring every 5 min.

 

 

Conclusion

Using two schedulers in a single SAP CPI iFlow provides a reliable way to handle both ad hoc and scheduled executions. It eliminates manual scheduler changes, reduces operational risks, and ensures that regular scheduled processing continues without interruption.

entry.title: Integration Suite: Building an MCP Server for a SAP CI Monitoring Assistant
entry.content:
Introduction

In SAP Integration Suite, Message Processing Logs (MPL) are the primary tool for monitoring and troubleshooting interfaces. Whenever something fails, the same routine follows: log into the tenant, open Monitor, filter by artifact, drill into the failed message, read the error. In my previous blogs I looked at ways to make this easier — automating email alerts for failed interfaces, logging custom header properties for better MPL search, and storing logs in Database for long-term retention.

Now we are taking it a step further. SAP recently released the MCP Server capability in Integration Suite, which lets you expose any API as a set of tools that an AI agent can discover and call at runtime. So instead of clicking through the Monitor, you can simply ask an assistant in natural language — "show me all failed messages in the last 2 hours" or "why did the XYZ interface fail?" — and let it query the tenant for you.

In this blog we will build a CI Monitoring Assistant: a generic, reusable MCP Server that sits on top of the SAP Cloud Integration OData API and can be consumed from any MCP-compatible client such as VS Code or Claude Desktop.

What is MCP?

Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that lets AI agents discover and invoke tools at runtime. You publish an MCP Server — a runtime endpoint that advertises a list of named, described tools — and the agent reads those descriptions to decide what to call and how. No hardcoding of API calls into the agent.

The problem we are solving

Standard MPL monitoring works, but it is entirely manual and click-driven. For operations teams this means:

  • Every check requires logging into the tenant and navigating the Monitor.
  • Correlating a failure across artifacts, error text and custom headers takes several drill-downs.
  • There is no easy way to ask a plain-language question and get a consolidated answer.

The SAP API Hub provides the OpenAPI JSON API specification for the SAP Cloud Integration OData API (MessageProcessingLogs). We retrieve that specification from SAP API Hub and convert it into OpenAPI 3.0 (converted using AI) so it can be used consistently by downstream tools and agents. The MCP Server then exposes the resulting read operations as tools, making monitoring conversational instead of manual.

Prerequisites

Before we start, make sure the following are in place:

  • Cloud Integration and API Management capabilities activated in Integration Suite.
  • The Integration Cell runtime enabled — go to Settings → Runtimes and confirm it shows Active. The MCP Server artifact runs on Integration Cell; it cannot be deployed to the classic Cloud Integration runtime.
    • MCP is available only in premium and enhanced edition plans, and in trial accounts.
  • The PI_Integration_Developer role collection assigned to your user in the BTP Cockpit.

If any capability or runtime is missing, activate it first — the MCP Server option will not appear until Integration Cell is running.

Solution overview

At a high level, the flow looks like this:

VS Code / Claude Desktop  (MCP client)
        |  MCP protocol
        v
   MCP Server artifact  (Integration Cell)
        |  HTTPS + CI OAuth
        v
   CI OData API  (MessageProcessingLogs)
        |
        v
   Your SAP Cloud Integration tenant

The MCP Server is created from an HTTP Endpoint with an OpenAPI 3.0 specification. We import the OpenAPI JSON that describes the monitoring operations, choose which operations become tools, add prompts and resources, configure the HTTPS adapter to authenticate to CI using OAuth, and deploy. The client then connects using a runtime service key.

Step-by-step guide

Step 1 – Enable the required capabilities

Activate Cloud Integration, API Management, and the Integration Cell runtime. Confirm under Settings → Runtimes that Integration Cell shows Active. This is the single most common blocker — the MCP Server artifact type will not be selectable otherwise.

Step 2 – Prepare the OpenAPI 3.0 JSON

The MCP Server needs an OpenAPI 3.0 specification. Each operationId in the spec becomes a tool name the agent can discover, and each description is what the agent reads to decide when and how to use the tool — so write the descriptions for the agent.

download - mcp-ci-message-processing-logs-openapi-3.0.json

Step 3 – Create the MCP Server artifact

Go to an existing or new Integration Package and create a new artifact of type: MCP Server. When prompted for the source type, choose HTTP Endpoint with OpenAPI Specification and upload the OpenAPI 3.0 JSON file you downloaded in Step 2. The wizard imports the spec and generates a list of tools from your operations.

Step 4 – Select tools, resources and prompts

  • Tools – select the operations you want to expose. Review the generated tool name and description for each; refine descriptions so the agent understands intent. (Max 15 tools can be selected per MCP Artifact)

  • Resources – add any static reference content the agent may need (for example, a list of interface names or a status-code cheat sheet). This is optional but improves answer quality.

  • Prompts – provide reusable prompt templates, e.g. "Summarise all failures in the last 2 hours grouped by interface." These give end users a guided starting point.

Step 5 – Create two runtime service keys (CI OData API + MCP Client Auth)

This solution needs two separate OAuth credentials, because two different calls happen and they use different Process Integration Runtime service plans:

  • CI OData Monitoring API (plan api) → used inside the MCP Server by the HTTPS receiver adapter to read Message Processing Logs.
  • MCP Client Authentication (plan integration-flow) → used by the AI client (VS Code / Claude Desktop) to authenticate to the deployed MCP Server endpoint.

Both are created from the Process Integration Runtime service in your BTP subaccount.

5a. Service key for the CI OData Monitoring API (plan: api)

This is the credential the HTTPS adapter uses in Step 6 to read monitoring data.

  1. In your BTP subaccount, go to Instances and Subscriptions → Create.
  2. Select service Process Integration Runtime, plan api and give the instance a name (e.g. it-rt-api-odata).
  3. On the Parameters step, add the monitoring-read role — MonitorDataRead (this grants read access to Message Processing Logs and related monitoring entities). Keep the grant type as Client Credentials and create it.
  4. On that instance, create a service key (e.g. it-rt-api-key-odata).
  5. From the service key, copy clientid, clientsecret, tokenurl (and the API url/host). These go into the Security Material referenced by the HTTPS adapter in Step 6.

Use only MonitorDataRead here — the assistant is read-only, so there is no reason to grant write or admin roles to the credential that reaches your monitoring data.

5b. Service key for MCP client authentication (plan: integration-flow)

This is the credential the AI client uses in Step 8 to call the deployed MCP Server endpoint on Integration Cell.

  1. In your BTP subaccount, go to Instances and Subscriptions → Create.
  2. Select service Process Integration Runtime, plan integration-flow and give the instance a name (e.g. it-rt-iflow-mcp).
  3. On the Parameters step, add the role API.invoke (required to invoke the deployed MCP endpoint). Keep the grant type as Client Credentials and create it.
  4. On that instance, create a service key (e.g. it-rt-iflow-key-mcp).
  5. From the service key, copy clientid, clientsecret and tokenurl . These are the credentials you enter in the MCP client (VS Code / Claude Desktop) to obtain an access token and call the MCP Server.

Step 6 – Configure the HTTPS adapter with CI OAuth

This is the authentication link between the MCP Server and the CI OData Monitoring API. Here you deploy the OAuth credential you created in Step 5a as a Security Material, then reference it in the HTTP receiver adapter of the MCP Server.

1. Deploy the Step 5a credentials as a Security Material - In Integration Suite, go to Monitor → Integrations and APIs → Manage Security → Security Material → Create → OAuth2 Client Credentials. Fill in the values from your Step 5a service key. Save and deploy it. This keeps the CI credentials inside Integration Suite, away from the agent.

2. Update the HTTP receiver adapter in the MCP Server - Open the MCP Server artifact and edit the HTTP receiver connection that points to the CI OData Monitoring API:

  • Set Authentication to OAuth2 Client Credentials.
  • In Credential Name, select the Security Material you just deployed.

Save the changes. The MCP Server will now use the Step 5a OAuth credential to authenticate every call it forwards to the CI Monitoring API.

Step 7 – Deploy the MCP Server

Once tools, resources, prompts and authentication are configured, deploy the artifact. Deployment provisions the runtime endpoint on Integration Cell and makes the tools available for discovery. Wait for the status to show as deployed/active. Note down the MCP Server endpoint URL — the MCP path must be unique.

Step 8 – Test with MCP Client (VS Code / Claude Desktop)

Now connect an MCP client. You need two things:

  • MCP Server URL – the endpoint of your deployed MCP Server. Get it from Monitor → Manage Integration Content on the Integration Cell runtime: open the deployed MCP Server artifact and copy its endpoint URL.
  • Authentication – the OAuth credentials from your Step 5b service key: clientid, clientsecret and tokenurl.

Add these to your MCP client configuration:

Claude Desktop – add the MCP Server to the configuration file with the endpoint URL and the Step 5b OAuth credentials (client ID, client secret, token URL). Restart Claude Desktop; the tools should appear as available

VS Code – add the MCP Server in your MCP settings (or your extension's config), pointing to the same endpoint URL and Step 5b credentials.

Once connected, the client fetches a token from the token URL using the client Id and secret, then uses it to call the MCP Server. The server discovers your tools, and you can start asking questions in natural language:

  • "List all failed messages in the last 2 hours."
  • "Show the error details for the last failure of the OrderToCash interface."
  • "Find messages where the PO number custom header equals 4500001234."

The agent picks the right tool, calls the MCP Server, which forwards the request to the CI Monitoring API using the OAuth credentials, and returns a consolidated answer — no clicking through the Monitor.

Future Enhancements

  • Extend to more OData APIs – The Cloud Integration OData API exposes far more than Message Processing Logs. Using the exact same steps in this blog, you can build multiple MCP Servers — each wrapping a different API — to create, deploy, undeploy, update, or delete artifacts, parameters, and security materials conversationally.
  • Govern write access carefully – This MCP server is deliberately read-only. The moment you expose write operations the agent can change your tenant, so grant each service key only the minimum role, keep it off production first, and add human confirmation before any destructive action.
  • No rate limiting or payload protection is added in this basic setup. For production, put the API Artifact / MCP Gateway governance layer in front (rate limiting, audit logging, IP blocking).
  • Move to the SAP MCP Hub – SAP is building a centralized, no-code MCP Hub (announced Q4 2025, planned GA in H1 2026) that removes the need for each team to build and maintain its own MCP Servers. It handles cross-cutting concerns like security, authorization, observability, and protocol versioning centrally, and adds no-code OData/OpenAPI-to-MCP conversion, modular servers that combine tools from multiple APIs, destination-based connectivity, AI-assisted tool optimization, built-in validation, and a test chat. Once available, migrating this assistant to the MCP Hub is the natural next step.
  • MCP on Integration Suite is a new capability and is evolving quickly; screens and options may differ slightly from what you see when you try it. Check the SAP Help Portal (Design APIs and MCP Servers) for the latest.

This blog provides a basic, reusable foundation. You can enhance the MCP Server based on your own requirements — more tools, tighter filters, governance policies, or write-back operations behind proper approval.

Reference

  • One Blog, Two Strategic Topics: API-Centric Integration and MCP Gateway

Conclusion

The MCP Server capability turns SAP Integration Suite monitoring from a manual, click-driven task into a conversational one. By exposing the CI OData Monitoring API as a small set of well-described tools, any MCP-compatible client — VS Code, Claude Desktop, or others — becomes a CI Monitoring Assistant that operations teams can query in plain language.

If you see scope for enhancement in the tool set or the OpenAPI spec, feel free to drop a comment. I would love to hear how you extend it.

Happy Integrating!

entry.title: Re: What is clean core anyway?
entry.content: In my experience the Clean Core conversation happens too late — usually after fit-gap workshops have already approved developments that should have been challenged at requirement stage. The distinction between a genuine SAP standard gap and a business preference dressed as a technical requirement is where most programmes lose Clean Core compliance before a single line of code is written. Has anyone found an effective way to enforce that challenge discipline in Design Authority without creating friction with the business?

entry.title: Re: Context Gravity vs. Data Gravity: Enabling actionable AI in distributed landscapes with SAP BDC
entry.content:
In my experience, technical teams often focus so much on the speed of the ML model that they underestimate the governance debt created by copying master data. It’s a common trap: you build a model on a replicated dataset, but once you need to connect that prediction to a real-world business decision—like a specific contract, SLA, or approval workflow—the 'truth' has already drifted.

Keeping the intelligence close to the authoritative SAP data, rather than trying to replicate that system elsewhere, is a much more sustainable architecture. It’s a great reminder that the business value isn't just in the prediction; it’s in the context that makes that prediction actionable. Thanks for sharing.

entry.title: Re: SAP Business AI in SAP Activate: Three Dimensions of AI for Your Implementation
entry.content:
Excellent framework, Jan. The shift toward AI-assisted implementation is inevitable, but the success of these dimensions really hinges on the 'Clean Core' foundation.

My observation in the field is that AI is a true force multiplier—if the underlying project data and architectural decisions are 'noisy,' these new tools will simply help us scale that complexity faster. The real value here isn't just the acceleration; it's the potential to use these AI assistants to enforce better governance and architectural discipline from the very beginning of the project. Getting the foundations right in the 'Realise' phase has never been more critical. Great to see the Activate methodology evolving this quickly.

entry.title: Re: What I Wish I Knew
entry.content:
I wish I had prioritized 'long-term operability' over 'go-live speed' from day one.

When you're early in your journey, the temptation is to treat the Go-Live as the finish line—to customize aggressively just to get the system running. But I learned the hard way that every line of unnecessary custom code is a 'technical tax' you’ll have to pay for years during upgrades and maintenance.

My advice to my former self: Spend as much time designing for maintainability and Clean Core standards as you do for the immediate business requirements. A simple, standard-compliant process that can evolve is worth infinitely more than a complex, highly-customized one that gets stuck in technical debt. The project doesn't end at go-live; that is actually when the real lifecycle begins.

entry.title: Re: How to determine current environment in CPQ?
entry.content: sleepynote, the DOMAIN tag simply returns our company name, which is the same for both environments. And I already referenced the script you suggested in my question, saying that it pulls the Deployment Setup table (which may or may not be correctly set up) and not the actual environment.

entry.title: Re: AI Agents vs. workflows - When to use what in SAP Sales and Service Cloud
entry.content:
This hybrid perspective is spot on. The real challenge, however, isn't just choosing between agents or workflows; it’s managing the interface between them.

In my experience, where most projects get into trouble is when these two layers evolve in isolation. If you have autonomous agents making decisions based on unstructured data, while workflows enforce rigid business rules, the risk of 'architectural drift' is high. Without a strong governance layer to bridge them, you can end up with a maintenance burden that outweighs the productivity gains.

The key is ensuring that both the agents and the workflows are tied back to the same authoritative business semantics. When they speak the same 'language' and respect the same core process rules, the hybrid approach becomes truly sustainable. Great read.

entry.title: Re: You may have outgrown your ERP systems - What can you do?
entry.content:
Great summary, Christine. Central Finance is undoubtedly one of the most powerful architectural moves for an organization navigating M&A or rapid growth.

However, in my experience, the technical 'connection' is only half the battle. The real success of a Central Finance implementation hinges on the data governance bridge between the source systems and the target. It’s easy to centralize data, but it’s much harder to harmonize the financial semantics across a heterogeneous landscape. If the master data isn't rigorous at the source, Central Finance risks centralizing the inaccuracy at scale.

For organizations looking to leverage this for AI or advanced innovation, that 'data harmonization' phase is the make-or-break moment. It’s less about the ERP upgrade and more about establishing a truly consistent financial truth. Thanks for laying out the strategic use cases.

entry.title: Re: Teaching SAP Without Teaching SAP
entry.content:
This is a profound shift in perspective, Matt. You’ve hit on the exact challenge we face in the professional implementation world as well.

We often see the same 'trap' in enterprise projects that you see in the classroom: teams focusing on feature adoption and 'doing the clicks' rather than understanding the underlying business intent. The best architects and consultants are exactly like the students you describe—they don’t just know how the system works; they know why the process is designed that way.

The example you gave about Segregation of Duties is spot on. When someone truly understands the 'why'—the risk, the control, the business outcome—they don't need a manual to tell them what to do. They build better, more sustainable systems because they aren't just configuring software; they are solving business problems.

It’s a great reminder that technology is merely the substrate for business logic, not the logic itself. Thanks for sharing.

entry.title: Re: Unused Budget per Planner from Previous Compensation...
entry.content:
Hi @David,

Thank you for your feedback. I took into consideration your recommendation about using the cascade budget approach. However, after further clarification with the client, we confirmed that the budget is assigned based on the Head of each Department/Division (SVP level), and the remaining budget should stay with that planner regardless of any future population changes.

I also noticed that, as you mentioned, the cascade budget approach requires significant manual effort. For example, some planners may not have more than two levels below them in the hierarchy, meaning the budget does not cascade automatically to the entire intended population.

Based on this, I was thinking about an alternative approach: calculate the remaining budget after the January cycle (Assigned Budget - Total Increase), export this information, and use it as the starting budget for the July cycle.

Have you seen this approach used in other implementations, or would you recommend a different way to handle this scenario?

entry.title: Re: Joule Custom MDF Redirection Request
entry.content:
Hi Maria,
Currently, there is one available Joule use case related to custom MDF objects which is "Approve MDF Workflow Requests". It supports workflow approvals for both custom and pre-delivered MDF objects. For more information, please refer to the Platform Use Cases Help Guide.
If you would like to see additional Joule use cases for custom MDF objects in future releases, we recommend submitting an enhancement request through the Customer Influence Portal under SAP SuccessFactors Platform, Foundation and Technology -> Metadata Framework (MDF) category. Enhancement requests are reviewed by the Product Management team and may be considered for future development and product releases based on customer demand and product strategy. For detailed instructions on how to submit an enhancement request, please refer to KBA 2090228.
In addition, you may want to explore Joule Studio, which provides capabilities for creating custom Joule experiences. We recommend evaluating whether it meets the requirements of your specific use case. To learn more, here you can find a few resources:
The Complete Joule Studio Resource Hub: Everything You Need to Get Started
Joule Studio, classic edition
We hope this information is helpful.

entry.title: Re: Predictive Accounting for incoming sales orders in S/4HANA
entry.content:
Dear SAP Support,

We have reviewed the official documentation for Predictive Accounting in SAP S/4HANA On‑Premise as well as SAP Notes 3223551 and 3653948.

Based on this information, our understanding is the following:

In SAP S/4HANA On‑Premise, predictive journal entries are only created correctly at the time of the goods issue.

The customer invoice only reduces an existing predictive journal entry and does not create a predictive entry if no goods issue has taken place.

There is no functionality in On‑Premise (including Fiori applications) to simulate or recalculate predictive journal entries by changing material prices.

Changing the material price in the master data does not update existing predictive entries; the prediction is only recalculated when the corresponding sales order item is changed.

Could you please confirm that in SAP S/4HANA On‑Premise 2025 the process flow Sales Order → Goods Issue → Customer Invoice is mandatory for the creation and reduction of predictive journal entries, and that the capability to generate predictive entries directly from the sales order without a goods issue (Order-Based Predictive Accounting) is available only in SAP S/4HANA Cloud and no longer part of SAP S/4HANA On‑Premise?  Best regards, Gustavo

entry.title: Re: Check number is not updating in Assignment filed of G...
entry.content:
Hello @Inthiyaz1 

Thank you for your question

 

In Public Cloud, the check number and journal entry assignment field are separate fields. There is no standard setting that automatically copies the check number into the Assignment field to the bank G/L line item for every payment and bank statement process.

For bank reconciliation, copying the value is usually unnecessary. Bank statement interpretation and matching function are provided so that you can use the Check Number directly. For example, supported interpretation logic can search open items by using an assignment number derived from the bank statement’s Customer Reference Number, Check Number, or memo line.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/19f2847b2d9548d29fa89996e1233126.html?locale=en-US

Here are steps you should consider,

Ensure you confirm whether the bank statement is available in the imported statement

In Reprocess Bank Statement Items, you can select the relevant house bank, statement, and item.

Ensure you check whether Check Number is populated in the bank statement details.

If the value is present, you can configure the reconciliation logic to use it directly rather than needing it in the G/L Assignment field. In the relevant bank statement processing rule or matching instruction, you can use Check Number (Bank Statement) as a source field for matching against the appropriate open item field, bank statements fields including Assignment Reference Check Number, Customer Reference Number, and End to End identification are added as available matching sources.

Ensure you verify the imported statement format mapping. If the bank provides the check number but the field is empty, you can review the mapping so that the source value is assigned to the bank statement Check Number field.  

You should only consider making use of Manage Substitution/Validation Rules to populate the standard Assignment field when the required source field and target are officially available in the relevant journal entry substitution context. Substitution rules for defined FI posting contexts, but this does not mean that the bank statement check number is available as a source for substituting Assignment during the generated bank posting.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c56f622a2edf491b9f1b596b55587009/5c8c2825535f4e3aa9b7f716a0085221.html?locale=en-US

Therefore, you do not assume that a substitution can perform this copy without first confirming the available fields.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud – Engineering Change Managem...
entry.content:
Good day @ParakhMahendiratta 

 

Thank you for your question

 

Question 1: Is this the standard behavior in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud?

Answer: Yes, in the delivered Change Record Discrete Industry process, the first is the approval of change request. The engineer provides the reason, affected objects, dates, attachments, and other information required for that decision. The change number is then generated for the implementation phase, where the BOM is changed with reference to that number

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f369b2eff700401494ba6e7c9a573288/3ba32a4dfb1e4a4e8d3bc288a1640ef0.html?locale=en-US

Question 2: If yes, does the approver only approve the engineering request rather than the actual BOM modifications?

Answer: At the initial approval stage, yes. The approver is primarily deciding whether the requested engineering work should proceed. The actual BOM component changes have not yet been implemented under the generated change number.

Question 3: Is there any standard process that allows the approver to review the proposed BOM changes before approval?

Answer: The better option is to separate request approval from implementation acceptance. After approval, you can start implementation, change the BOM with the generated change number and future valid from date, then route the change record through an acceptance or completion step. Change records workflow can control process steps from initiation through acceptance and execution, responsible users and permitted actions.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f369b2eff700401494ba6e7c9a573288/f397ae39479844f78b100d79aea35a51.html?locale=en-US

The approver can open the assigned BOM from Manage Change Records and review its component information. However, the system does not take record of a configurable option that moves standard change number generation before the initial request approval while retaining the delivered process semantics. The configuration activity Change Number handling at Change Record Item controls handling by item type, you cannot redesign the approval sequence.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f369b2eff700401494ba6e7c9a573288/d1c63dbe742b45ad98efa0e726da74a5.html?locale=en-US

Here is a practical controlled process:

Ensure you create and approve the change request, then start implementation and generate the change number.

In Maintain Bills Material, Version 2, ensure you assign the change record or change the number and enter the future valid from date.

You can implement additions, deletions, quality changes, and replacements and send the record to one approver for acceptance.

Ensure you close or release the implementation only after approval. BOM supports BOM maintenance and assignment of change records.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f369b2eff700401494ba6e7c9a573288/68ace4fb90454696863aa9041d27c646.html?locale=en-US

 

Question 4: Is it possible to configure the workflow so that:

  • the Change Number is available before approval,
  • the engineer prepares the BOM changes,
  • and the approver reviews the modified BOM before approving and releasing it?

Answer: You can use Maintenance of Bills of Material Items. It is used for editing or deleting items across multiple BOMs, simulation before execution, and synchronous or background processing.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f369b2eff700401494ba6e7c9a573288/702c12d24ac34a37b7a6bacfd6bd6176.html?locale=en-US

Question 5: If this is not supported in the standard Public Cloud process, what is the SAP-recommended approach for customers who require approval of the actual engineering changes rather than only the request?

Answer: The better sequence is approval, change number generation, simulation, controlled mass execution, result review in Monitor mass Maintenance of Bills Material, and final acceptance.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Power BI Data Refresh through SAP BW Process Chain
entry.content:
Introduction

In many SAP BW projects, data loading is orchestrated by SAP BW process chains, while business users consume the data through Microsoft Power BI dashboards. One common requirement is to automatically refresh the Power BI dataset immediately after the BW process chain finishes successfully.

While the Power BI Service exposes REST APIs for triggering dataset refreshes, these APIs are not available in Power BI Report Server. As a result, a different approach is required.

In this blog post, I will demonstrate a simple solution for triggering a Microsoft Power BI Report Server data refresh directly from an SAP BW process chain.

 

 

Solution Overview

The implementation consists of the following steps:

  1. Create a data flow in SAP BW.
  2. Create a SQL Server stored procedure that triggers the Power BI dataset refresh.
  3. Develop an ABAP report that executes the stored procedure.
  4. Add the ABAP report to an SAP BW process chain.

 

 

Sample Scenario

For demonstration purposes, I created a simple end-to-end data flow. This is the flow I've developed:

  1. Excel [Local]
  2. DSO [BW]
  3. Query [BW]
  4. Dashboard [Power BI]

 

 

Creating Stored Procedure in Power BI Report Server

The Power BI dashboard used in this example is named "Dashboard to Test PBI Data Refresh".

A scheduled refresh has already been configured for this dashboard. Instead of waiting for the scheduled execution time, we can trigger the refresh by calling the AddEvent procedure in the Report Server database.

The following stored procedure encapsulates this logic.

USE [ReportServer]
GO

SET ANSI_NULLS ON
GO

SET QUOTED_IDENTIFIER ON
GO

CREATE OR ALTER PROCEDURE [dbo].[MyRefreshData]
AS
BEGIN
SET NOCOUNT ON;

DECLARE @DASHBOARD_NAME  NVARCHAR(100),
@DASHBOARD_ID    VARCHAR(100),
@SUBSCRIPTION_ID VARCHAR(100),
@RETURN_VALUE    INT

SET @DASHBOARD_NAME = 'Dashboard to Test PBI Data Refresh'

SET @DASHBOARD_ID = (
 SELECT TOP 1 [ItemID]
 FROM [ReportServer].[dbo].[Catalog]
 WHERE [Name] = @DASHBOARD_NAME
)

SET @Subscription_ID = (
 SELECT TOP 1 SubscriptionID
 FROM [ReportServer].[dbo].[ReportSchedule]
 WHERE [ReportID] = @DASHBOARD_ID
)

WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:3'

EXEC @RETURN_VALUE = [ReportServer].[dbo].[AddEvent]
 @EventType = N'DataModelRefresh',
 @EventData = @Subscription_ID

END

Note: Replace "Dashboard to Test PBI Data Refresh" with the name of your own Power BI report.

 

 

Executing Stored Procedure from SAP BW

Next, we need an ABAP report that executes the stored procedure.

Before running the report, create a secondary database connection to the SQL Server database using transaction DBCO.

The following ABAP report opens the database connection, executes the stored procedure, and then closes the connection.

REPORT ZPBI_DATA_REFRESH.

DATA: LV_DBCO  TYPE DBCON_NAME.

LV_DBCO = 'SQL206'.

*Open database connection
EXEC SQL.
  CONNECT to :LV_DBCO
ENDEXEC.

IF SY-SUBRC <> 0.
  MESSAGE 'Error in opening the connection' TYPE 'E'.
ENDIF.


*Execute the proceudre
EXEC SQL.
  execute reportserver.dbo.myrefreshdata
ENDEXEC.

IF SY-SUBRC <> 0.
  MESSAGE 'Error in executing the procedure' TYPE 'E'.
ENDIF.


*Close database connection
EXEC SQL.
  DISCONNECT :LV_DBCO
ENDEXEC.

IF SY-SUBRC <> 0.
  MESSAGE 'Error in closing the connection' TYPE 'E'.
ENDIF.

 

 

Adding ABAP Program to the Process Chain

The final step is to execute the ABAP report automatically from the SAP BW process chain.

Create an ABAP Program process variant, assign program ZPBI_DATA_REFRESH, and place it after the successful completion of your BW data loading processes.

With this configuration, every successful execution of the process chain automatically triggers the Power BI Report Server dataset refresh, ensuring that business users always see the latest data without any manual intervention.

 

 

Conclusion

This approach provides a straightforward integration between SAP BW and Power BI Report Server by leveraging a SQL Server stored procedure and a simple ABAP report. If you are using Power BI Report Server together with SAP BW, this technique offers a reliable way to synchronize your ETL process with Power BI data refreshes.

entry.title: Re: GR Label Printing Based on “Number of Labels” Field
entry.content:
Hello @kkumara 

 

Thank you for your question

 

Yes, Public Cloud supports your requirement. For the goods receipt label printing, your user can manually enter the required value in the Number of Labels field. When a manual value is entered, the system uses that value instead of recalculating the label count from the goods receipt quantity.

See the following reference for more insights

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/53b92207de734253ab34ff5200fd372b.html?locale=en-US

For your example

  1. Goods receipt quantity: 100
  2. Number of labels: 2
  3. Expected output: 2 labels

Here are steps to consider:

In Post Goods Receipt for Purchasing Document, ensure you enter the purchase order and select the relevant item.

You can enter the received quantity, in the printing section and change Printing from No print to the required option, such as individual slip.

Ensure you enter 2 in the number of labels and post the goods receipt.

Before testing, verify the product master data. The Label Type and Label Form fields should be maintained in the plant specific product data before goods receipt can be printed.

Here are items you should check: Purchase order and item, material, goods receipt quantity 100, printing section, and number of labels.

After you have posted, open Display Material Document List and select the material.

You can choose More—Extra—Output and confirm that the generated Goods Receipt label output contains two labels and capture the output status and PDF preview. Output review function inventory Management output control is made available. You cannot control the count through Output Parameter Determination or printer copy settings to determine the output type, channel, printer, and form template, while the business label quantity is supplied during goods receipt entry.

See the following references for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/b7f02d1514ff4a44b144ea214556fdaf.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/b7f02d1514ff4a44b144ea214556fdaf.html?locale=en-US

If it still prints 100 Labels after entering 2, ensure you confirm that the generated output is Goods Receipt label, rather than a custom form that repeats records according to the received quantity. The custom layout or its data binding would then need correction.

  

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Is there any way to get the value of material master...
entry.content:
Good day @Iris59 

Thank you for your question

Your observation is correct. Note that in Public Cloud, the released CDS view I ProductUnitsOfMeasure exposes standard unit of measure information such as alternative unit, numerator, denominator, weight, volume, and EAN data. It does not expose the batch specific unit indicators corresponding to the MARM fields you sent such as:

  • LUn for leading Batch specific Unit of Measure
  • VUn for Valuation Based on Batch specific unit of Measure

The standard CDS field lists confirms that these indicators are not part of the released interface.

Having direct access to table MARM is also permitted in custom development in the cloud environment. Custom CDS views and ABAP Cloud developments can consume only objects released for the relevant extensibility use case, normally with C1 release status.

See the following references for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/6aa39f1ac05441e5a23f484f31e477e7/993c8fc86d3c4d8687688d74577fb6a5.html?locale=en-US

The indicators themselves are supported functionally. Lun is maintained for the leading batch specific unit and that Vun identifies a unit relevant for batch specific valuation. The limitation is specifically the absence of these fields from a released interface.

See the following references for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/75ffb753128eb44ce10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US

Here are objects you should verify:

In View Browser, search for I ProductUnitsOfMeasure and review the Element and Annotation tabs.

You can search additionally for terms such as Batch specific Unit, Leading Unit, and Valuation Unit.

Ensure you confirm whether any new release specific CDS view exposes the required indicators.

See the following references for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/0bde695751505c08e10000000a441470.html?locale=en-US

If no released object contains these fields, then there is currently no supported standard method to read them in custom code. A custom CDS view cannot add fields that are absent from its released source.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Commodity Code values not displayed in CDS view I_PUR...
entry.content:
Good day @Michelle1975 

 

Thank you for your question

 

Your observation is correct. The released CDS view I_PurchaseOrderItemAPI01 does not provide the purchase order item’s commodity code as an exposes field. The fact that the commodity code is visible in the purchase order does not mean that it is automatically available in every released purchase order CDS interface. I_PurchaseOrderItemAPI01 is the released purchase order item view, but its public field scope is fixed.

See the following references for more details:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/6d471dfc743048c3891601fa8ab0dc1e.html?locale=en-US

In the standard purchasing process, the commodity code is displayed at purchase order item level under Material Data. For a product-based item, It is derived from the product classification and cannot be changed directly in the purchase order. For an item without a product number, you can enter it manually.

See the following references for more details:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/6dd96f68c48e4c139cda7b1bac5599b1/0b5bcd3c64c24040aab6f6bfb7424daa.html?locale=en-US

Here are areas you should consider:

When your requirement is to retrieve the commodity code assigned to the product, you can use a released product classification CDS view rather than expecting it from the purchase order item view. SAP provides Product Commodity Code Data Extraction, technical name I_ProdCommodityCodeDEX for retrieving products and their assigned commodity codes. The view is C1 released.

See the following references for more details:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/ccf21a569e484f7ab65a7ecaea508081.html?locale=en-US

A custom query can combine:

  • I_PurchaseOrderItemAPI01 for purchase order, item, product, plant, and document information
  • I_ProdCommodityCodeDEX for the commodity code assigned to the product.

You should design the join and date logic carefully because commodity code classification can be time dependent and may depend on the relevant numbering scheme or country. You should not assume that product’s current commodity code always represents the exact value applicable when an older purchase order was created.

In View Browser, search for I_PurchaseOrderItemAPI01 and ensure you confirm that the commodity code is not listed under commodity code fields.

Search for I_ProdCommodityCodeDEX and you can check its release status, supported capabilities, product key, validity fields, numbering scheme, and commodity code fields.

You can create custom CDS view only if both sources support the intended extensibility scenario.

 

 Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 2 — Roll & Move
entry.content:
1. game-service.js

const cds = require('@sap/cds')

module.exports = class GameService extends cds.ApplicationService {
  async init() {

    const { Players, BoardSquares } = this.entities

    // ── startTurn ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Waiting]  →  CAP validates status before this runs
    // @TO: #Playing      →  CAP updates status after this returns
    // No business logic needed — CAP's @flow.status handles the transition.
    this.on('startTurn', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // Intentionally empty: the @from/@to annotations do all the work.
    })

    // ── rollDice ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Playing]  →  CAP validates status before this runs
    // @TO: #Moving       →  CAP updates status after this returns
    this.on('rollDice', 'Players', async (req) => {
      const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
      if (!player) return req.error(404, 'Player not found')

      const roll = Math.ceil(Math.random() * 6)
      let newPosition = player.position + roll

      // Can't overshoot square 100 — must land exactly
      if (newPosition > 100) {
        await UPDATE(Players).set({ lastRoll: roll }).where({ ID: player.ID })
        const needed = 100 - player.position
        return req.reject(409, `Need exactly ${needed} to finish. Rolled ${roll}. No move.`)
      }

      // Resolve snake or ladder on the new square
      const square = await SELECT.one.from(BoardSquares).where({ square: newPosition })
      let finalPosition = newPosition
      let event = null

      if (square?.ladderTo) {
        finalPosition = square.ladderTo
        event = { type: 'ladder', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
      } else if (square?.snakeTo) {
        finalPosition = square.snakeTo
        event = square.isDoubleHead
          ? { type: 'doubleSnake', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
          : { type: 'snake', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
      }

      // Persist position and last roll
      await UPDATE(Players)
        .set({ position: finalPosition, lastRoll: roll })
        .where({ ID: player.ID })

      // Emit domain event so subscribers (Week 3) can react
      if (event) await this.emit('BoardEvent', { playerID: player.ID, ...event })

      // Use req.reply() — not return — to pin the response value before CAP's
      // @flow.status after-handler runs. A plain `return` gets cleared by the
      // flow machinery, producing HTTP 204 instead of the expected 200 + body.
      req.reply({ roll, position: finalPosition, event: event?.type ?? 'normal' })
    })

    // ── confirmMove ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Moving]  →  normal end of a turn, hands off to next player (Week 3)
    // @TO: #Waiting     →  player waits for their next turn
    this.on('confirmMove', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // Week 3 will add turn rotation here.
      // For now: just let CAP apply @TO: #Waiting
    })

    // ── blockPlayer ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Moving]  →  called when player lands on a double-headed snake
    // @TO: #Blocked     →  player skips their next turn
    this.on('blockPlayer', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // Business logic: the position was already updated in rollDice.
      // This action just transitions state — CAP handles the @TO: #Blocked update.
      req.info('Player is blocked and will skip their next turn.')
    })

    // ── unblockPlayer ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Blocked]   →  called at the start of a blocked player's next turn
    // @TO: $flow.previous →  CAP restores the status to whatever it was before #Blocked
    this.on('unblockPlayer', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // CAP's $flow.previous does the work — no code needed here.
      // The player's status returns to #Waiting automatically.
    })
    
    //---skipturn------------------------------------
    this.on('skipTurn', 'Players', async (req) => {
      const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
      await UPDATE(Players)
      .set({ turnsBlocked: (player.turnsBlocked || 0) + 1 })
      .where({ ID: player.ID })
    })

    // ── winGame ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Moving]  →  called when player.position === 100
    // @TO: #Finished    →  player has won
    //this.on('winGame', 'Players', async (req) => {
      //const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
      //if (!player) return req.error(404, 'Player not found')

      // Mark the session as finished with this player as winner
      //await UPDATE('snakeladder.GameSessions')
        //.set({
          //finishedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
          //winner_ID: player.ID
        //})
        //.where({ ID: player.session_ID })

      //await this.emit('GameWon', { playerID: player.ID, sessionID: player.session_ID })
    //})

    // ── winGame ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
// @from: [#Moving]  →  called when player.position === 100
// @TO: #Finished    →  player has won
this.on('winGame', 'Players', async (req) => {
  const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID });

  if (!player) {
    return req.error(404, 'Player not found');
  }

  // Ensure the player has reached the last square
  if (player.position !== 100) {
    return req.error(409, 'Player has not reached square 100');
  }

  // Mark the game session as finished
  await UPDATE('snakeladder.GameSessions')
    .set({
      finishedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
      winner_ID: player.ID
    })
    .where({ ID: player.session_ID });

  // Emit the GameWon event
  await this.emit('GameWon', {
    playerID: player.ID,
    sessionID: player.session_ID
  });

  return {
    message: 'Congratulations! You won the game.'
  };
});

    return super.init()
  }
}

 

2. Response 409 for rolldice from wrong state

3. Position after DoubleSnake

4. wingame

currently, the status is directly turned to finished if wingame is called without checking players position. But, I have added the validation to even check whether current position is 100 or not to reach finished state.

entry.title: Re: SAC Email Server Configuration Error
entry.content:
Dear NilsDre,

Hope you are well. Thank you for your message.

To address this scenario effectively, it is recommended reviewing the following points to ensure a resolution:

  1. When connecting to the SMTP email server, whether the same issue would also occur when choosing the Authentication Type as Basic?
  2. When choosing the Authentication Type as OAuth, whether the Refresh Token field has been populated automatically after clicking on the Get Token button?
  3. When choosing the Authentication Type as OAuth, whether the "Envelope Sender Address" is the same as "User Name"? If yes, whether it is a valid email account?

Thank you for your time.

Best wishes,

Lena

entry.title: Re: Can we send the current user email address in a SAP F...
entry.content:
Good day @S0008017772 

 

Thank you for your question

In Public Cloud, the settings for Web Services, allows you to define the OCI catalog URL and it’s call structure. The call structure can pass fixed values or supported SAP fields to the catalog. SY-UNAME is the standard field for passing the current username,

For example:

Parameter name: USERNAME

Parameter value: SY-UNAME

Parameter type: SAP Field

This passes the user ID not the user’s email address.

See the following references for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/spmm/5825852732.html?locale=en-US

I am not aware of any call structure that resolves the current user’s business email address. The available field selection is fixed by the delivered OCI integration. You cannot enter an arbitrary CDS field or Business Partner email field and expect to resolve it dynamically.

See the following references for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/41dc8457e0d7b576e10000000a4450e5.html?locale=en-US

I am not aware of key user functionality that enriches the outbound OCI catalog call with additional user specific parameters such as email address. Therefore, an on-premises enhancement or unreleased BAdI would not be available in this Public Cloud environment.

Here are practical options:

You can check whether Amazon Business can identify the user using the user ID. Ensure you configure the required Amazon parameter using SY-UNAME, then maintain a corresponding user mapping in Amazon Business.

You can use a common fixed email or account value, only if Amazon accepts a shared purchasing identity. Ensure you maintain the amazon parameter as a fixed value rather than a field.

If Amazon strictly requires the individual email address, you can raise an SAP incident or feature request under the OCI purchasing application component, ensure you include confirmation from Amazon that the email should be supplied during the punch out request and you cannot derive it from the supplied user ID.

For testing, Open Settings for Web Services, select the Amazon web service, and review Call Structure.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris
